revision.text:
[[ar:تنصيب إمبراطور اليابان]][[be:Інтранізацыя імператара Японіі]][[en:Enthronement of the Japanese emperor]][[fa:بر تخت نشستن امپراتور ژاپن]][[id:Pelantikan Kaisar Jepang]][[it:Intronizzazione dell'imperatore del Giappone]][[ja:即位の礼]][[pt:Entronização do Imperador do Japão]][[ru:Интронизация императора Японии]][[simple:Enthronement of the Japanese emperor]][[th:พิธีขึ้นครองราชย์ของจักรพรรดิญี่ปุ่น]][[uk:Інтронізація Імператора Японії]][[vi:Lễ đăng quang của Thiên hoàng Nhật Bản]][[zh:即位之禮 (日本)]] {{Short description|Ceremony of the Imperial House of Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=August 2022}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2017}} {{Infobox recurring event | name = Enthronement of the Japanese emperor | native_name = {{nobold|{{lang|ja|即位の礼}}}} | native_name_lang = ja | logo = | logo_alt = | logo_caption = | logo_size = | image = Ceremony of the Enthronement of His Majesty the Emperor at the Seiden7.jpg | image_size = 300px | alt = | caption = Enthronement of Emperor Naruhito in Tokyo Imperial Palace in 2019 | genre = | date = | begins = | ends = | venue = | location = [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]], Matsu-no-Ma Hall | coordinates = | country = {{JPN}} | years_active = | first = {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (First) [[Emperor Kanmu]] (Modern version) | founder_name = | last = | prev = [[2019 Japanese imperial transition#Enthronement Ceremony|22 October 2019]]
{{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} | next = | participants = *{{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} and [[Empress of Japan|Empress]] *{{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}} adult members *Heads of the three branches of government **{{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} **[[Speaker of the House of Representatives (Japan)|Speaker of the House of Representatives]] **[[President of the House of Councillors]] **[[Chief Justice of Japan|Chief Justice of the Supreme Court]] *Foreign heads of state *Domestic invitees | attendance = | capacity = | area = | budget = | activity = | leader_name = | patron = | filing = | people = | member = | sponsor = | website = [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/eindex.html The Imperial Household Agency] [https://japan.kantei.go.jp/ongoingtopics/_00008.html Cabinet Public Relations Office, Cabinet Secretariat] | current = | footnotes = }} [[File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|thumb|Enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|qid=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō|1=Emperor Taishō}} in [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] in 1915.]] [[File:Enthronement of Emperor Showa 1.JPG|thumb|Enthronement of Emperor Shōwa in Kyoto Imperial Palace in 1928.]] [[File:Emperor Showa.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa|1=Hirohito}} at his enthronement in 1928.]] [[File:Emperor_Akihito_199011_1.jpg|thumb|right|Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}} wearing the [[sokutai]] at the enthronement ceremony in November 1990.]] {{nihongo|Enthronement|即位の礼|Sokui no rei}} is an ancient ceremony that marks the accession of a new {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} to Japan's {{ill|Chrysanthemum Throne|en|Chrysanthemum Throne|qid=Q1981690|lt=Chrysanthemum Throne|1=Chrysanthemum Throne}}, the world's oldest continuous {{ill|Hereditary monarchy|en|Hereditary monarchy|qid=Q849242|lt=Hereditary monarchy|1=Hereditary monarchy}}. Various ancient {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=imperial regalia|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} (three sacred treasures) are given to the new sovereign during the course of the rite. It is the most important out of the [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace]]. The [[2019 Japanese imperial transition#Enthronement Ceremony|most recent enthronement]] took place on 22 October 2019 to enthrone Emperor {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}. ==Enthronement ceremonies== The enthronement ceremony consist of five sub-ceremonies, which are conducted as constitutional functions ([[:ja:国事行為|国事行為]]) based on Article 3 of the {{ill|Constitution of Japan|en|Constitution of Japan|qid=Q237082|lt=Constitution of Japan|1=Constitution of Japan}} as follows:{{cite web| url=https://www.kantei.go.jp/jp/content/kihonhoushin.pdf |date=April 3, 2018 |title=閣議決定：天皇陛下の御退位及び皇太子殿下の御即位に伴う国の儀式等の挙行に係る基本方針について |trans-title=Cabinet decision: Basic Principles for conducting state ceremonies etc. regarding to abdication of His Majesty the Emperor and enthronement of His Imperial Highness |website=[[Prime Minister's Official Residence (Japan)|Prime Minister's Office of Japan]] |access-date=February 26, 2021 }} ===Presentation of the Three Sacred Treasures=== [[File:"Inheritance Ceremony of Kenji and others.jpg|thumb|left|Presentation of the sword ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]-no-Tsurugi'' and the ''[[:en:Yasakani no magatama|Yasakani no magatama]]'' at the enthronement of Emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}}, 1989]] The first is the simplest, ''Kenji-tō-Shōkei-no-gi'' (剣璽等承継の儀), it takes place immediately after the death or abdication of the preceding sovereign. The successor is formally presented with boxes containing two of the three items that compose the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}: (1) a replica sword representing the sword ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]-no-Tsurugi'' (lit. "Grasscutter Sword") (草薙劍), though the original is allegedly enshrined at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|qid=Q11751|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya}}; and (2) the ''[[:en:Yasakani no magatama|Yasakani no magatama]]'' (八尺瓊曲玉), a necklace of [[Magatama|comma-shaped stone beads]]. The third and most important of the items of the regalia is the mirror ''[[Yata no Kagami]]'' (八咫鏡), which is enshrined in the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] as the ''[[shintai|go-shintai]]'' (御神体), or the embodiment of the [[Amaterasu|Sun goddess]] herself. It is permanently housed in the shrine, and is not presented to the emperor for the enthronement ceremony. Imperial messengers and priests are sent to this shrine, as well as to the tomb-shrines of the four emperors whose reigns immediately preceded his, to inform them of the new emperor's accession. The three items of the imperial regalia were originally said to have been given by the Sun goddess, [[Amaterasu]], to her grandson when he first descended to earth and became the founder of the imperial dynasty. Unlike other monarchies, Japan has no crown in its regalia. In the {{ill|2019 Japanese imperial transition|en|2019 Japanese imperial transition|qid=Q44023338|lt=2019 enthronement ceremony of Naruhito|1=2019 Japanese imperial transition}}, the treasures were presented to the new emperor in the morning of his ascension date. The visits to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] by Imperial messengers and [[Kannushi|priest]]s, as well as to the tombs of the previous four emperors, continued on as in past enthronements. ===The first audience after the enthronement=== The second is called ''Sokui-go-Choken-no-gi'' (即位後朝見の儀). The new emperor will meet the three chiefs of {{ill|Separation of powers|en|Separation of powers|qid=Q79896|lt=the tripartite political system|1=Separation of powers}} as the representatives of citizens for the first time. ===Proclaiming enthronement at the main palace=== [[File:Imperial_throne.jpg|thumb|''Takamikura'' used for the enthronement]] [[File:1990 enthronement banzai banner 萬歳旛.svg|thumb|[[Ten thousand years#Japan|Banzai]] banner used for the enthronement ceremony.]] The third part of the ceremony, called ''Sokuirei-Seiden-no-gi'' (即位礼正殿の儀), is the ritual to proclaim and congratulate the enthronement. It is the core part; the last such ceremony was held on October 22, 2019, for {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}. This ancient rite was traditionally held in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, the former capital of Japan; beginning in 1990, it is done in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}. The 1990 enthronement of {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}} was the first to be covered on television, and have [[Imperial Guard (Japan)#Imperial Guard of the National Police Agency, 1947–present|Imperial Guard]]s in political traditional uniforms. It was done indoors, with the elevated stand placed inside the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] complex. Only part of the ritual is public, and the regalia itself is generally seen only by the emperor and a few [[Shinto]] priests. First came a three-hour ceremony in which the new emperor ritually informed his ancestors that the enthronement is about to take place. This was followed by the enthronement itself, which takes place per tradition in an enclosure called the ''Takamikura'', containing a great square pedestal upholding three octagonal pedestals topped by a simple chair. This was surrounded by an octagonal pavilion with curtains, surmounted by a great golden {{ill|Fenghuang|en|Fenghuang|qid=Q527525|lt=Phoenix|1=Fenghuang}}.{{cite news|url=http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,928214-1,00.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090113204444/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,928214-1,00.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=January 13, 2009 |title=Emperor Enthroned |magazine={{ill|Time (magazine)|en|Time (magazine)|qid=Q43297|lt=Time|1=Time (magazine)}} |date=November 19, 1928 |access-date=October 12, 2008}} At the same time, the empress, in full dress regalia, moved to a smaller adjacent throne beside her husband's. Traditional drums were, at this point of the ceremony, beaten to start the proceedings.{{cite web |title=Japan emperor to announce enthronement in ancient-style ceremony |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20191022/p2g/00m/0na/014000c |website=Mainichi Daily News |language=en |date=October 22, 2019 |access-date=October 22, 2019 |archive-date=October 22, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191022104040/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20191022/p2g/00m/0na/014000c |url-status=dead }} The new emperor proceeded to the chair, where after being seated, the ''Kusanagi'', ''Yasakani no Magatama'', {{ill|Privy Seal of Japan|en|Privy Seal of Japan|qid=Q2915131|lt=privy seal|1=Privy Seal of Japan}} and {{ill|State Seal of Japan|en|State Seal of Japan|qid=Q1187512|lt=state seal|1=State Seal of Japan}} were placed on stands next to him. A simple wooden [[:en:Shaku (ritual baton)|sceptre]] was presented to the emperor, who faced his {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=prime minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} standing in an adjacent courtyard, representing the Japanese people. The emperor offered an address announcing his accession to the throne, calling upon his subjects to single-mindedly assist him in attaining all of his aspirations. His prime minister replied with an address promising fidelity and devotion, followed by a "[[Ten thousand years#Japan|three cheers of Banzai]]" from all of those present. The timing of this last event was precisely synchronized, so that Japanese around the world could join in the "Banzai" shout at precisely the moment that it was being offered in Kyoto or in Tokyo. This moment of the rite ends with the firing of a {{ill|21-gun salute|en|21-gun salute|qid=Q49095233|lt=21-gun salute|1=21-gun salute}} by the {{ill|Japan Self-Defense Forces|en|Japan Self-Defense Forces|qid=Q275488|lt=Japan Self-Defense Forces|1=Japan Self-Defense Forces}}. After this, both seals and the two regalia items are carried off the imperial pavilion. ===Celebration parade=== [[File:Imperial Procession by motorcar after the Ceremony of the Enthronement2019(2).png|thumb|left|Celebration parade at {{ill|Aoyama, Tokyo|en|Aoyama, Tokyo|qid=Q1193420|lt=Aoyama, Tokyo|1=Aoyama, Tokyo}}, 2019]] The fourth, called ''Shukuga-Onretsu-no-gi'' ({{nihongo2|祝賀御列の儀}}), is the {{ill|motorcade|en|motorcade|qid=Q1367880|lt=motorcade|1=Motorcade}} procession. The emperor and empress, now back to wearing formal wear, are then both driven through midst Tokyo toward {{ill|Akasaka Estate|en|Akasaka Estate|qid=Q11635414|lt=Akasaka Estate|1=Akasaka Estate}} by the state limousine ([[:ja:御料車|御料車]]) to acknowledge the cheers of the ordinary citizens on the major streets of the capital who have assembled there. As head of state, he also receives the first salutes on that segment by a guard of honour of the [[:en:Japan Self-Defense Force|Japan Self-Defense Force]] and the {{ill|Imperial Guard (Japan)|en|Imperial Guard (Japan)|qid=Q707181|lt=Imperial Guard Headquarters of the National Police Agency|1=Imperial Guard (Japan)}} ([[:ja:皇宮警察本部|皇宮警察本部]]).{{cite web|date=December 6, 2019 |url= https://www.kantei.go.jp/jp/headline/kouikeisyou_gishikitou/onretsunogi.html |title= 祝賀御列の儀 |trans-title=Celebration parade |website=[[Prime Minister's Official Residence (Japan)|Prime Minister's Office of Japan]] |access-date=February 24, 2021 }} In the case of {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}}, the parade was postponed to 10 November 2019, with consideration about the enormous damage of {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|qid=Q69841946|lt=Reiwa 1 East Japan Typhoon|1=Typhoon Hagibis}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.gov-online.go.jp/sp/gosokui/en/index.html |date=2019 |title=Announcement on the Ceremonies of the Accession to the Throne of His Majesty the Emperor |website=[[Cabinet Office (Japan)|Cabinet Office, Japan]] |access-date=February 25, 2021 }} As the state limousine, a specially coach worked [[Toyota Century#Century Royal (G51; 2006)|Toyota Century Royal]] was prepared.{{cite web|url=https://www.motor1.com/news/371825/custom-toyota-century-convertible-emperor/ |date=2019 |author=Christopher Smith |title=Custom Toyota Century Convertible Ready To Serve Japan's New Emperor |website=motor1 |access-date=February 27, 2021 }} In this ceremony, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} and constables of the Imperial Guard Headquarters provided guards of honor and security while the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department|en|Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department|qid=Q1312559|lt=Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department}}'s Police band and {{ill|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Music Corps|en|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Music Corps|qid=Q11664287|lt=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Music Corps|1=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force Music Corps}} played suitable music.{{cite web |date=November 10, 2019 |url=https://www.nippon.com/ja/news/p01091/ |title=令和の「祝賀御列の儀」フォトドキュメント |trans-title=Photodocument of Shukuga-Onretsu-no-gi of Reiwa |website=Nippon.com |access-date=March 18, 2021 }} ===Court banquet=== The fifth is the official banquet, called ''Kyouen-no-gi'' (饗宴の儀), which is the occasion to announce and celebrate the enthronement, and to receive the felicitations of the guests to be held within [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]]. ==Another banquet== A banquet is hosted by the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} and their spouse in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} to thank the foreign heads of State and other dignitaries for visiting Japan, and to foster a greater understanding of Japan by presenting displays of traditional Japanese culture.{{cite web|url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2019/03/19/national/japan-invite-guests-195-nations-events-marking-enthronement-new-emperor/ |date=March 19, 2019 |title=Japan to invite guests from 195 nations for events marking enthronement of new Emperor |website=[[The Japan Times]] |access-date=February 25, 2021 }} This banquet is an event out of the scope of constitutional functions (国事行為). == History of the enthronement ceremony == [[File:Benkan_emperor_komei.jpg|thumb|250x250px|The {{ill|Benkan|en|Benkan|qid=Q124412016|lt=crown|1=Benkan}} of Emperor Komei. He was crowned only for the coronation.]] [[File:Kone.jpg|thumb|250x250px|{{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Komei's|1=Emperor Kōmei}} {{ill|Mianfu|en|Mianfu|qid=Q709163|lt=Mianfu|1=Mianfu}}. It is the emperor's dress.]] As far as the records in the [[Kojiki|Kiki]] are concerned, the coronation ceremony was held on the first day of the year, but it is believed that when the calendar system was imported from the {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|qid=Q7405|lt=Sui dynasty|1=Sui dynasty}} during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, the accession to the throne on the first day of the year was adopted, following the example of the continental emperors.{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=169-170}} Prior to this, the coronation ceremony was integrated with the [[:en:Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]], in which the new emperor appeared before his subjects after receiving the spiritual authority of [[Amaterasu]] at a festival the day after the [[:en:Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]] was held on the winter solstice (around November in the {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}). At this point, the emperor's succession to the throne was announced to the world through the presentation of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=170-172,176}} Later, the accession ceremony was moved up to New Year's Day and preceded the [[Daijosai|Daijō-sai]]. Although the details of the ceremony are not clear even in the Chronicles, the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Divine Instruments|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} were first given and received, and the ceremony of ascending to the "platform" was held on the same day or later.{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=29-30}} The first time the rituals were recorded in detail was on New Year's Day 1, the fourth year of {{ill|Shuchō|en|Shuchō|qid=Q777413|lt=Vermilion Bird|1=Shuchō}} (February 14, 690). It is the coronation ceremony of {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}.{{Efn|In this example, the emperor had been [[shosei]] (acting in state affairs) for more than three years before succeeding to the throne, and on this day he formally succeeded to the throne.}} At this time, the ceremony is as follows{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=27-28}} * [[Isonokami no Maro|Isonokami no Maro]] establishes the {{ill|Shield|en|Shield|qid=Q131559|lt=Big Shield|1=Shield}}. * Nakatomi Oshima, Count of Shingi recites the Tenjin Jyushi. * Imaizumi Irochi consecrates the sacred [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] and [[Yata no Kagami]]. * The one hundred lords and ladies of the court assemble and perform an eightfold [[kashiwate]]. At this point, the Tenjin Jyushi had been recited twice, along with the Dai-namesai. In the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} (720), it is also stated that {{Quote frame|On the day of {{ill|Senso (ritual)|lt=senso|ja|践祚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senso (ritual)|1=senso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4504500}}, the sermon of Tenjin is performed and the mirror and sword of the Imbibe's divine regalia are raised}}It is clear that the transfer of sacred artifacts took place after the jubilee.{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=32-33}} However, at the time of the succession to the throne of [[Emperor Kanmu]] in the first year of the reign of 781, the enthronement was followed by the Imperial Rescript at [[Daikoku-den]] (大黒殿) which effectively separated the {{ill|Senso (ritual)|lt=Sensō|ja|践祚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senso (ritual)|1=senso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4504500}} from the enthronement. In the 8th year of extension (930), the {{ill|Senso (ritual)|lt=Sensō|ja|践祚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senso (ritual)|1=senso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4504500}} and Enthronement were effectively separated.{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|pp=33-34}} Furthermore, when {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|qid=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} succeeded to the throne in 930, {{ill|Senso (ritual)|lt=Sensō|ja|践祚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senso (ritual)|1=senso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4504500}} and enthronement were explicitly separated. In the 870s, the Teikan Ritual and the 11th century Eke Yosei, the accession ceremony was clearly defined as follows. * The Emperor faces south with a ladle in his high seat. * The Emperor faces the south on his high throne, holding a ladle. * Kashiwate, dancing, and congratulating the Emperor and his hundred officials, and three cheers given by the military officials. Here, the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code's|1=Yōrō Code}} provisions for the transfer of the Sword and Seal and the Tenjin jubilee were removed, and the ceremony took on a more continental style, with the {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|qid=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven|1=Son of Heaven}} facing south and wearing ceremonial dress.{{Sfn|Mayumi|2019|p=33}} However, by the middle of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, this form had already broken down, and the pseudo-attendants in the palace (in the early Heian period, the head of the palace was called the "prince's deputy" because the prince was in charge, and there were a total of six people on either side, consisting of three deputy attendants and a shonagon) were joined by the uchi-ben (equivalent to a superior lord), sotoben (an official who attended the ceremony in general, but only those who were designated to do so), and sotoben (an official who attended the ceremony in general, but only those who were designated to do so). Only nominated persons stand. No one below the Rank of a nobleman is allowed to attend. One of the sotoben became the missionary. (From the Middle Ages to the early modern period, it was common for the soto-ben to consist of two each of the daimon, chunagon, and councilors.) In the Middle Ages and early modern times, the sotoben were usually two each from the daionnagon, chunnagon, and counselor. Only a limited number of nobles and officials, such as those in charge of ceremonies, participated in the coronation ceremony, and nobles who did not have that role, even ministers, watched from inside the draperies on either side of the high throne. The coronation ceremony of {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|qid=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō|1=Emperor Go-Ichijō}} on February 7, 1016 (Chōwa 5), was conducted by {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|qid=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|en|Fujiwara no Sanesuke|qid=Q3090659|lt=Fujiwara no Sanesuke|1=Fujiwara no Sanesuke}}, a major figure in the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Royal court|1=Royal court}}, wrote in detail in his diary, "Shōyūki", because Sanesuke was able to observe the coronation ceremony as a spectator, not as a participant. In addition, from the late Heian period (after {{ill|Emperor Horikawa|en|Emperor Horikawa|qid=Q464267|lt=Emperor Horikawa|1=Emperor Horikawa}}) onward, the regent was in the middle or lower tier of the high palace, and the sekibaku in [[Sokutai]] in the east curtain of the north classic aisle, behind the high palace (to the right when viewed from the south). The regent of {{ill|Emperor Go-Ichijō|en|Emperor Go-Ichijō|qid=Q374087|lt=Emperor Go-Ichijō|1=Emperor Go-Ichijō}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}, was also in this position, so it is highly likely that early regents were in this position as well.) As a rule, throughout the Heian period, the venue for ceremonies was the Daigoku-den Hall of the Chodo-in (the eight sects), but due to the burning of the Daigoku-den, {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|qid=Q349280|lt=Emperor Yōzei|1=Emperor Yōzei}} used the Fengakuden Hall. Due to illness, {{ill|Emperor Reizei|en|Emperor Reizei|qid=Q374125|lt=Emperor Reizei|1=Emperor Reizei}} ascended the throne in the Shichinden Hall of the [[Heian Palace#Inner Palace .28Dairi.29|Interior]], and due to the burning of the Daigoku-den, {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|qid=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō|1=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} moved to the {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|qid=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} used Hall of Capital in the Inner Palace. Since the Daigoku-den Hall fell into disuse after the Great Fire of Angen in 1176, all emperors from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} to {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|qid=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}} of the mid {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, with the exception of {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|qid=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami|1=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|qid=Q464039|lt=Chōkei|1=Emperor Chōkei}}, and {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|qid=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama|1=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the [[Southern Court]], who were not in Kyoto. After {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|en|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|qid=Q349452|lt=Go-Kashiwabara|1=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara}}, the Grand Council of State was not rebuilt, and the Hall of Capital was used, where it remained until {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}. In the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages|1=Middle Ages}} and later (the first example is said to be {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|qid=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō|1=Emperor Go-Sanjō}}, but it is said to have become customary after {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|qid=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa|1=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}), a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}}-style ceremony called the Accession ceremony was also held. This style was continued until the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Although the coronation ceremony is one of the most important {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Emperor|1=Imperial House of Japan}} events, the {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|en|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|qid=Q349452|lt=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|1=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara}} of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} 1500 (Meiou 9), but was unable to perform the ceremonies, and in 1521 (Daiei Gen), he performed the coronation ceremony in his 22nd year on the throne, based on assistance from the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|qid=Q1154406|lt=Muromachi shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} and others. The direct trigger for the ceremony was the shogunate's donation of 20,000 rolls of rice, but in reality, it was not possible to carry out the ceremony with just that money, as the shogunate had donated money several times since the {{ill|Bunki|en|Bunki|qid=Q1324885|lt=Bunki|1=Bunki}} era, and the Imperial Court had accumulated tools prepared with donations from Honganji and others. {{ill|Emperor Go-Nara|en|Emperor Go-Nara|qid=Q349467|lt=Emperor Go-Nara's|1=Emperor Go-Nara}} accession to the throne was postponed for about ten years due to lack of funds, even though there were many items left over from the accession of {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|en|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|qid=Q349452|lt=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|1=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara}}. In the case of {{ill|Emperor Ōgimachi|en|Emperor Ōgimachi|qid=Q334076|lt=Emperor Ogimachi|1=Emperor Ōgimachi}}, the imperial treasury was unable to contribute to the cost of the coronation, and the ceremony was held with the assistance of {{ill|Mōri Motonari|en|Mōri Motonari|qid=Q1156545|lt=Mōri Motonari|1=Mōri Motonari}}. However, despite many difficulties, the ceremony has been performed without fail in every generation, except for {{ill|Emperor Chūkyō|en|Emperor Chūkyō|qid=Q357253|lt=Emperor Chūkyō|1=Emperor Chūkyō}}, who only reigned for a short time due to the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|qid=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War|1=Jōkyū War}}. After the Jokyu Rebellion, when {{ill|Emperor Shijō|en|Emperor Shijō|qid=Q374020|lt=Emperor Shijō|1=Emperor Shijō}} ascended to the throne, spectators crowded into the garden, and even after this, there are articles in the court nobles' diaries that suggest that the rituals were hindered by spectators. On the occasion of the accession to the throne of {{ill|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|en|Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|qid=Q349452|lt=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara|1=Emperor Go-Kashiwabara}}, it is said that spectators gathered "like a haze of clouds. In keeping with this tradition, recent research has shown that during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the general public was able to watch the accession ceremony at the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]] by purchasing stamps (a kind of ticket).{{Cite web |url=http://osaka.yomiuri.co.jp/inishie/news/is61118c.htm |title=天皇即位式、江戸時代は庶民の人気行事 ニュース 列島いにしえ探訪 文化 伝統 関西発 ''Yomiuri Online''（読売新聞） |access-date=February 10, 2022 |archive-date=November 20, 2006 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061120164638/http://osaka.yomiuri.co.jp/inishie/news/is61118c.htm |url-status=dead }} Nomiya Sadaharu, who was not involved in the ceremonies at the time of the accession to the throne of {{ill|Empress Go-Sakuramachi|en|Empress Go-Sakuramachi|qid=Q230525|lt=Empress Go-Sakuramachi|1=Empress Go-Sakuramachi}} but only attended the ceremony to see the empress off, was upset that many ordinary court nobles were not allowed to observe the ceremony because of the large number of "samurai slaves" and "miscellaneous people", but that the shogunate's envoys were allowed to see the ceremony unofficially in the Capital Hall. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], a series of ceremonies related to the {{ill|Senso (ritual)|lt=senso|ja|践祚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senso (ritual)|1=senso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4504500}} and accession of the emperor were established by the enactment of the former Imperial Household Law and the Tengoku Order. In addition, there are a number of other rituals and ceremonies that can be performed in Kyoto. The current {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}}, enacted in 1947 ([[:en:Shōwa (1926-1989)|Shōwa]] 22), does not specify the location of the ceremonies; the [[:en:Heisei|Heisei]] and [[:en:Reiwa|Reiwa]] accession ceremonies were held at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, with the former name "Shinden no Gi" replaced by "Shoden no Gi". The ceremony began with an announcement of the due date to [[Amaterasu]], the ancestor deity of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Emperor|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and successive emperors, followed by a report to the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Three Palaces]] in the Imperial Palace, and an imperial proclamation to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. In accordance with the Tengoku Order, the period from {{ill|Spring (season)|en|Spring (season)|qid=Q1312|lt=Spring|1=Spring (season)}} to {{ill|Autumn|en|Autumn|qid=Q1314|lt=Autumn|1=Autumn}} is designated as the time of accession, and for one year after the {{ill|Demise of the Crown|en|Demise of the Crown|qid=Q842994|lt=Collapse|1=Demise of the Crown}} of the previous emperor, the accession ceremony and the [[:en:Daijō-sai|Daijō-sai]] are not held as a period of mourning. This period of mourning is called "Ryoami" in Japanese. The most important ceremony of the Coronation is the Shoden Ceremony, in which the Emperor wears a [[Sokutai]] sash and the Empress wears a {{ill|Jūnihitoe|en|Jūnihitoe|qid=Q2418740|lt=Jūnihitoe|1=Jūnihitoe}}. The emperor's sash is called Kourouzen-no-ohô, which can only be worn by the emperor. The {{ill|Throne|en|Throne|qid=Q189233|lt=Throne|1=Throne}} used in the Shoden ceremony is called "Takamikura" for the emperor and "Michodai" for the empress. It is built on three layers of black-painted sandalwood with an octagonal roof, and decorated with {{ill|Fenghuang|en|Fenghuang|qid=Q527525|lt=Phoenix|1=Fenghuang}}s, {{ill|Mirror|en|Mirror|qid=Q35197|lt=Mirror|1=Mirror}}s, and other ornaments. It is 5.9 meters high, 6 meters wide, and weighs 8 tons. === Emperor Meiji's enthronement === [[File:明治天皇・神宮親謁.jpg|thumb|207x207px|Emperor Meiji's audience at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]]]] Since the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tempō calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}} was in use at the time of the coronation of the 122nd and Meiji Emperor, and the dates differ from the current {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}, they are listed in the order of the Tenpō calendar (Gregorian calendar). On 13 February 1867 ([[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]]: December 25, {{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|qid=Q776775|lt=Keio|1=Keiō}} 2), following the death of the 121st Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Komei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}, his son, {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}} Mutsuhito succeeded to the throne, becoming the 122nd Emperor of Japan. Initially, the accession ceremony was scheduled to take place in November, but it was postponed due to the many difficulties in the country's affairs amidst the rapid changes in the times, including the return of power by [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]]. In May of the following year, 1868, in order to proclaim the arrival of a new era, the new Meiji government decided to hold a new coronation ceremony appropriate to the changes, and appointed the {{ill|Tsuwano Domain|en|Tsuwano Domain|qid=Q3034561|lt=Tsuwano Domain|1=Tsuwano Domain}} lord and Department of The {{ill|Tsuwano Domain|en|Tsuwano Domain|qid=Q3034561|lt=Tsuwano Domain|1=Tsuwano Domain}} and deputy governor of the [[Department of Divinities]], Kamei Korekan, was appointed as the Imperial Accession Ceremony Coordinator. [[Iwakura Tomomi]] ordered Kamei to abolish the Tang-style rituals and restore the old style. As a symbol of the new era, the {{ill|Globe|en|Globe|qid=Q133792|lt=Globe|1=Globe}} was used in the ceremony to make the emperor's prestige known to the world. Since the Takamiza used for the accession to the throne of Emperor Takamyo had been lost in the fire that destroyed the inner palace in the first year of {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|qid=Q1327259|lt=Ansei|1=Ansei}} (1854), the ledger used for the annual festivities was called Takamiza. Since the costumes and decorations that were considered to be in the Tang style were completely abolished, the ceremonial dress was abolished and the sash, which had been the second formal dress after the ceremonial dress since the Heian period, was used. Flags of honor in the garden were also abolished and replaced by the {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}} flag. On August 17, Keio 4 (gregorian calendar: 2 October 1868), he announced that his coronation would take place ten days later, on 27 August (12 October 1868), and related ceremonies began on August 21. On the 27th, the next day after the imperial envoy was sent to {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|qid=Q349287|lt=Emperor Sutoku|1=Emperor Sutoku}} to read out the decree in front of his spirit on the 26th of the same month, the anniversary of his death, and on the day of the coronation, the decree was read out by the decree envoy, the first person in attendance read out the [[Norito]], and an ancient song was sung. Then they all chanted "Hai" and the ceremony ended. * Meiji-no-dairei - total cost 43,800 {{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|qid=Q3057942|lt=Ryō|1=Ryō}} ** Ritual of sending an imperial envoy to Ise Shrine, August 21, 1868 (October 6, 1868) August 21, 1868 (October 6, 1868) ** Imperial envoy dispatched to Emperor Jimmu's tomb, Emperor Tenchi's tomb, and the tomb of the previous three emperors. August 23 (October 8, the same year) ** Enthronement ceremony ** Enthronement ceremony: August 27 (October 12) * The Daijō-sai was held in Tokyo on November 17, 1871 (December 28, 1871). === Emperor Taisho's enthronement === {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|qid=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taisho|1=Emperor Taishō}} was enthroned on 10 November 1915 at the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]. {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|qid=Q1197656|lt=Empress Teimei|1=Empress Teimei}} was absent because she was pregnant with their fourth child ({{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|qid=Q280229|lt=Takahito, Prince Mikasa|1=Takahito, Prince Mikasa}}). === Emperor Showa's enthronement === {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa|1=Hirohito}} was enthroned on 10 November 1928 at the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace]]. === Emperor Akihito's enthronement === {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Emperor Akihito|1=Akihito}} was enthroned on 12 November 1990 at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]]. === Emperor Naruhito's enthronement === {{more|2019 Japanese imperial transition#Enthronement Ceremony}} {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Emperor Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} was enthroned on 22 October 2019 at the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace]]. ==The Daijō-sai== {{Main|Daijosai}} {{Expand Japanese|大嘗祭|date=December 2021}} {{Split section|date=December 2021}} [[File:Emperor Akihito Daijōsai(1990).jpg|thumb|The Daijō-sai of Emperor Akihito in 1990]] The ''Daijō-sai'' (大嘗祭) is a special religious service conducted in November after the enthronement, in which the emperor thanks peace of mind and rich harvest to the {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|qid=Q511056|lt=Solar deity|1=Solar deity}} [[Amaterasu]] (天照大神) and surrounding deities, and pray for Japan and its citizens. From [[Shinto]] viewpoint, the emperor is believed to be united to deity Amaterasu in a unique way to share in her {{ill|Divinity|en|Divinity|qid=Q1379405|lt=Divinity|1=Divinity}}. In general, the Daijosai is considered as a kind of thanksgiving harvest festival, in the same way as [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai (新嘗祭)]] is conducted annually on November 23, a {{ill|Public holidays in Japan|en|Public holidays in Japan|qid=Q1195354|lt=public holiday|1=Public holidays in Japan}} of {{ill|Labor Thanksgiving Day|en|Labor Thanksgiving Day|qid=Q82405|lt=Labor Thanksgiving Day|1=Labor Thanksgiving Day}}. Actually, in the year the Daijō-sai is held, the Niiname-sai (新嘗祭) is not held. {{cite book|author=Tsunetada Mayumi |author-link=:ja:真弓常忠 |title=大嘗祭 |trans-title=Enthronement of the Japanese emperor |publisher=[[Chikuma Shobō]] |series=Chikuma Shinsyo |year=2019 }} The Emperor and Empress both perform the Daijosai ceremony in November after ascending the throne in a partly televised ceremony and since 2019 it is a livestreamed event. It is only performed once during their reign. {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}} performed it on 22-23 November 1990 and {{ill|Naruhito|en|Naruhito|qid=Q217096|lt=Naruhito|1=Naruhito}} on 14-15 November 2019. The Emperor offers gifts such as {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}, {{ill|kelp|en|kelp|qid=Q1055316|lt=kelp|1=Kelp}}, {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|qid=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}} and {{ill|Abalone|en|Abalone|qid=Q4857439|lt=Abalone|1=Abalone}} to the gods. Then he reads an appeal to the gods and eats the offering and prays. The Emperor and Empress perform the rites separately. It takes about 3 hours. Over 500 people are present including the {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}}, government officials, representatives of state and private sector firms, society groups and members of the press. It originates as a [[Shinto]] rite from at least the 7th century. It is held as a private event by the Imperial Household, in order that it does not violate the {{ill|Separation of church and state|en|Separation of church and state|qid=Q753623|lt=Separation of church and state|1=Separation of church and state}}. A special complex with over 30 structures (大嘗宮, ''daijōkyū'') are built for the event. Afterward, they are accessible to the public for a few weeks and then dismantled. In 1990, the ritual cost more than 2.7 billion yen ($24.7 million).{{Cite web |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Japan-s-Reiwa-era/Japan-emperor-performs-centuries-old-succession-rite |author=Yukihiro Enomoto |title=Japan emperor performs centuries-old succession rite |magazine=[[Nikkei Asian Review]] |access-date=September 24, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200923130825/https://asia.nikkei.com/Spotlight/Japan-s-Reiwa-era/Japan-emperor-performs-centuries-old-succession-rite |archive-date=September 23, 2020}} '''Details'''
First, two special [[:en:Rice paddies|Rice paddies]] (斎田, ''saiden'') are chosen and purified by elaborate [[Shinto]] purification rites. The families of the farmers who are to cultivate the rice in these paddies must be in perfect health. Once the rice is grown and harvested, it is stored in a special Shinto shrine as its ''go-shintai'' (御神体), the embodiment of a [[kami]] or divine force. Each kernel must be whole and unbroken, and is individually polished before it is boiled. Some [[sake]] is also brewed from this rice. The two sets of rice seedlings now blessed each come from the western and eastern prefectures of Japan, and the chosen rice from these is assigned from a designated prefecture each in the west and east of the country, respectively. Two [[:en:Thatched roof|Thatched roof]] two-room [[:en:Hut (dwelling)|hut]]s (悠紀殿 ''yukiden'', lit. East-region hall) and (主基殿 ''sukiden'', lit. West-region hall) are built within a corresponding special enclosure, using a native Japanese building style that predates and is thus devoid of all [[:en:Culture of China|Chinese]] cultural influence. The Yukiden and Sukiden represent the east and west halves of Japan, respectively. Each hall is divided into two rooms, with one room containing a large {{ill|Couch|en|Couch|qid=Q131514|lt=Couch|1=Couch}} made of tatami mats at its center, in addition to a seat for the emperor and a place to enshrine the kami; the second is used by musicians. All furniture and household items also preserve these earliest, and thus most purely Japanese forms: e.g., all {{ill|Pottery|en|Pottery|qid=Q11642|lt=Pottery|1=Pottery}} objects are fired but unglazed. These two structures represent the house of the preceding emperor and that of the new emperor. In earlier times, when the head of a household died his house was burned; before the founding of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, whenever an emperor died his entire capital city was burned as a rite of purification. As in the earlier ceremony, the two houses represent housing styles from western and eastern parts of Japan. Since 1990, the temporary enclosure is located at the eastern grounds of the Imperial Palace complex. [[File:Igimono1.JPG|thumb|Ritual items from the enthronement of the Japanese emperor]] After a ritual {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=bath|1=Bathing}}, the emperor is dressed entirely in the white silk dress of a Shinto priest, but with a special long train. Surrounded by {{ill|Courtier|en|Courtier|qid=Q1511216|lt=Courtier|1=Courtier}}s (some of them carrying torches), the emperor solemnly enters first the enclosure and then each of these huts in turn and performs the same ritual—from 6:30 to 9:30 PM in the first, and in the second from 12:30 to 3:30 AM on the same night. A {{ill|Mat|en|Mat|qid=Q1136834|lt=Mat|1=Mat}} is unrolled before him and then rolled up again as he walks, so that his feet never touch the ground. A special {{ill|Umbrella|en|Umbrella|qid=Q41607|lt=Umbrella|1=Umbrella}} is held over the sovereign's head, in which the shade hangs from a phoenix carved at the end of the pole and prevents any defilement of his sacred person coming from the air above him. Kneeling on a {{ill|Mat|en|Mat|qid=Q1136834|lt=Mat|1=Mat}} situated to face the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]], as the traditional [[gagaku]] court music is played by the court orchestra the emperor makes an offering of the sacred rice, the sake made from this rice, {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|qid=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, fish and a variety of other foods from both the land and the sea to the kami, the offerings of east and west being made in their corresponding halls. Then he eats some of this sacred rice himself, as an act of divine communion that consummates his singular unity with Amaterasu-ōmikami, thus making him (in Shinto tradition) the {{ill|intermediary|en|intermediary|qid=Q1666223|lt=intermediary|1=Intermediary}} between Amaterasu and the Japanese people. He then prays to the gods in gratitude, and then leaves the huts.{{cite news| url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1990-11-23-mn-5212-story.html | work=[[Los Angeles Times]] | first=Karl | last=Schoenberger | date=November 23, 1990 | access-date=May 2, 2010 | title=Akihito in Final Ritual of Passage}} == Gallery == File:霊元天皇即位・後西天皇譲位図屏風.jpg|Enthronement of {{ill|Emperor Reigen|en|Emperor Reigen|qid=Q313808|lt=Emperor Reigen|1=Emperor Reigen}} File:Enthronement Ceremony of Emperor Kōmei.png|{{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}} File:Enthronement of Emperor Taisho 1.JPG|[[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]] File:Enthronement of Emperor Hirohito.jpg|{{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Showa|1=Hirohito}} File:Kone.jpg File:Benkan emperor komei.jpg|{{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}}'s {{ill|Benkan|en|Benkan|qid=Q124412016|lt=Benkan|1=Benkan}} File:Raikan Raifuku.jpg|Pre-Meiji period ceremonial outfit of civil mandarins File:Raikan raifuku.jpg|Pre-Meiji period ceremonial outfit of civil mandarins File:Buraikan.jpg|Pre-Meiji period ceremonial outfit of military mandarins File:Sokutai-Kurohou for male attendants.JPG File:Ceremony of Enthronement1.jpg File:威儀の者(Military officer).png File:衛門(gatekeeper).png File:Sokutai-Akehou for the bearers of ceremonial items.JPG File:Sokutai-Hanadahou for the bearers of ceremonial items.JPG File:Draft of Daijōkyu.jpg|Mockup, layout plan for pavilions in the Daijōkyū for the Daijōsai ceremony in November 2019. File:Special Parade of the Ceremonial Horse-Drawn Carriages2.JPG|Kareta, Special Parade of the Ceremonial Horse-Drawn Carriages 2009 == See also == * [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace]] ==Annotations== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} ===Works cited=== * {{cite book |first=Tsunetada |last=Mayumi |author-link=:ja:真弓常忠 |title=大嘗祭 |trans-title=Enthronement of the Japanese emperor |publisher=Chikuma Shobō |series=Chikuma Shinsyo |year=2019 |isbn=978-4480099198 }} ==Further reading== *{{cite web|author=Saitō Katsuhisa |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/japan-topics/c06112/pomp-and-pageantry-emperor-naruhito%E2%80%99s-enthronement-ceremony.html |date=October 21, 2019 |title=Pomp and Pageantry: Emperor Naruhito's Enthronement Ceremony |website=nippon.com }} *{{ill|Robert S. Ellwood|en|Robert S. Ellwood|qid=Q4531279|lt=Robert S. Ellwood|1=Robert S. Ellwood}}, ''The Feast of Kingship: Accession Ceremonies in Ancient Japan'' (Tokyo: Sophia University, 1973). *{{ill|D. C. Holtom|en|D. C. Holtom|qid=Q20675538|lt=D. C. Holtom|1=D. C. Holtom}}, ''Japanese Enthronement Ceremonies: With an Account of the Imperial Regalia'' (Tokyo: Sophia University, 1972). *John Breen and Mark Teeuwen, New history of Shinto (Wiley-Blackwell, 2010), pp. 168–198. ==External links== *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yeAiigw0Xb0 Video of Emperor Naruhito's enthronement ceremony] {{Coronation}} [[en:Enthronement of the Japanese emperor]] [[ar:تنصيب إمبراطور اليابان]] [[be:Інтранізацыя імператара Японіі]] {{Commons category|Ceremonies of the Accession to the Throne}} [[fa:بر تخت نشستن امپراتور ژاپن]] [[id:Pelantikan Kaisar Jepang]] [[it:Intronizzazione dell'imperatore del Giappone]] [[ja:即位の礼]] [[pt:Entronização do Imperador do Japão]] [[ru:Интронизация императора Японии]] [[simple:Enthronement of the Japanese emperor]] [[th:พิธีขึ้นครองราชย์ของจักรพรรดิญี่ปุ่น]] [[uk:Інтронізація Імператора Японії]] [[vi:Lễ đăng quang của Thiên hoàng Nhật Bản]] [[zh:即位之禮 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q4202178}} [[Category:Ceremonies in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial Rituals]] [[Category:Japanese monarchy]] [[Category:Regalia]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
